title: Greek salad
subtitle: The proper village salad: no lettuce, big rough cuts, a slab of feta on top.
excerpt: Horiatiki, the village salad of summer Greece. Ripe tomatoes, cucumber, raw onion, green pepper, kalamata olives, all in big rough cuts, with a slab of feta over the top and oregano scattered. No lettuce. Olive oil drunk straight off the plate.
sourceNotes:
Greek home cookery, particularly the rural village version that gives the dish its proper name, horiatiki. Documented in Greek and Cypriot cookbooks throughout the twentieth century.

The lettuce-free composition is the defining feature; the dish was tinkered into a lettuce-base form for hotel and tourist menus in the 1960s and 1970s, which is what most people outside Greece were served. The village original is the older form and the version that holds up.

Quality of tomato, cucumber, and feta carries the dish entirely. Out of season, find another salad.

content.text: The salad you get in a Greek village is not the lettuce-and-feta plate served in hotels. The proper version is a heap of rough-cut tomato, cucumber, raw onion, green pepper, and a few kalamata olives, with a slab of feta the size of a beer mat laid over the top. Dried oregano gets scattered; olive oil gets poured; salt goes on once at the end. The point is to keep biting through to the feta and mopping the plate with bread.
The Greek name is
horiatiki
, meaning of the village. Outside Greece, restaurants serve the lettuce-base version that grew up around tourist resorts in the 1960s. The village original is older and simpler.
The salad is a summer dish. Out of season, when tomatoes taste of nothing and cucumbers are watery, the whole construction falls apart. Wait.
What you need

items.name: Extra-virgin olive oil
items.prepNote: a fruity Greek or Cretan oil

items.name: Dried oregano
items.prepNote: Greek wild oregano if you have it

content.text: You need a wide shallow bowl rather than a deep one. The salad should sit in a single layer so the feta stays on top.
Method
Cut everything large

Cut {{tomato}} of tomatoes into wedges or thick rounds, depending on size; a cherry tomato halves, a beefsteak makes eight wedges. Slice {{cucumber}} cucumber lengthways into quarters and chop into thick chunks. Cut {{pepper-green}} green pepper into rings or strips. Slice {{red-onion}} red onion into thin half-moons. Big rough cuts; this is not a chopped salad.
Build the bowl

Tip the vegetables into the bowl. Scatter {{olives-kalamata}} of kalamata olives across the top. Warn anyone eating that the stones are still in; that is the proper way to serve them and the flavour is better for it.
Lay the feta on top

Sit {{feta}} of feta in one piece on top of the salad. Do not crumble it; the slab is part of the visual signal and lets the eater break off shards as they go. A two-hundred-gram slab the size of a beer mat is right for four. The feta wants to be a proper Greek or Bulgarian sheep's-milk variety, briny and slightly tangy; the bland industrial cubes do not work.
Oil, vinegar, oregano

Drizzle {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil across the salad in a slow zigzag, hitting the feta as much as the vegetables. Add {{red-wine-vinegar}} of red wine vinegar if you want a brighter edge; the tomato juice carries enough acid on its own and many Greek cooks skip it. Scatter {{oregano-dried}} of dried oregano over the feta. Finish with a pinch of salt.
Serve with bread to mop
The salad goes to the table with country bread and the rest of the meal. The olive oil and tomato juice pool at the bottom of the bowl; that puddle is the prize. Mop with bread, eat with the hands.

attrs.intro: The salad turns on the ingredients. Most failures trace to a substitution.

items.fix: Build the salad on the plate as the rest of the meal is served. A salt-then-wait approach concentrates flavour but is not traditional; for a stronger version, lightly salt the tomato wedges five minutes before assembling, then tip out the water.
items.cause: Tomatoes or cucumbers were underripe or held too long after cutting.

items.fix: Look for a Greek or Bulgarian sheep's-milk feta with PDO labelling, sold in a slab in brine. Cypriot feta and Turkish beyaz peynir also work; both are tangier than mass-market versions.
items.cause: Industrial cubed feta or a non-PDO supermarket brick.

items.fix: Slice the onion thinly and toss with a pinch of salt and the vinegar five minutes before tipping into the bowl; the bite mellows. A milder white onion or a couple of spring onions also work.
items.cause: Onion sliced thick or left too long without salt.

items.fix: Greek salad wants a fruity, peppery extra-virgin. A flat oil leaves a flat plate; spend on better oil if anything.
items.symptom: Olive oil leaves a flat puddle.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting

content.text: Variations

Add capers, a handful of purslane, or sliced radish to a Cretan-style horiatiki. Some Aegean island cooks crumble dried wild thyme instead of oregano; some add a little dried mint. The structural rule (no lettuce, big cuts, slab of feta) stays the same.
For a vegan version, swap the feta for a slab of grilled aubergine seasoned with thyme and lemon. Not horiatiki any more, but a good summer plate on the same shape.
Where this dish lives

Horiatiki is the daily summer salad of rural Greece, sat on tables next to grilled fish, lamb chops, or chips at lunch and dinner from June to September. The dish moved into the resort-and-hotel circuit in the post-war years and got lettuce-bottomed in the translation, which is what most non-Greek diners have eaten. The village version stayed where it was.

Feta itself is the only Greek cheese with European protected designation status; PDO rules require Greek sheep's milk with up to thirty percent goat. Outside that bracket, what arrives in a supermarket fridge labelled feta-style is something else, and it shows on the plate.
